title: Defiance of Tyrants Must Be the New Republican Litmus Test
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman argues that nullification of unconstitutional federal acts is the necessary backbone of any meaningful Republican policy, presenting a Biblical, Constitutional, and historical case against the doctrine of unconditional submission to the federal government. He calls for ending the careers of GOP officials like Asa Hutchinson and Greg Treat who have blocked state-level defiance of Roe v. Wade.

questions.question: Why does the author claim nullificationism is the form of every Republican policy at its testing point?
questions.answer: Borrowing C.S. Lewis's framing of courage as the form of every virtue at the testing point, Silberman argues that without the willingness to enforce Republican policy in defiance of federal overreach, every other Republican commitment becomes hollow because it will only be expressed when easy.
questions.quote: Reworking Lewis’ point for the political situation we find ourselves in, it must be said that nullificationism is not simply one of the Republican policies, but the form of every Republican policy at the testing point.

questions.question: How does Silberman characterize the conservative legal establishment's relationship to federal power?
questions.answer: He says conservative political and legal communities have grown alongside the federal leviathan and embraced ex cathedra federal supremacy, denying any role for the states in interpreting the Constitution.
questions.quote: As the federal government has ballooned into a leviathan that is no longer a recognizably American institution, conservative political and legal communities have gone with it, embracing a philosophy of federal *ex cathedra* rule by rejecting all State-level agency in Constitutional interpretation.

questions.question: What Biblical argument does the article use to reject unconditional submission to civil authority?
questions.answer: The article cites John Knox and the examples of the Hebrew midwives, Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, and the apostles in Acts 5 to show that while submission to authority is biblical, magistrates forfeit their authority when they command disobedience to God.
questions.quote: But likewise true it is, that in things which they commit against His glory, He has commanded no obedience, but rather, He has approved, yea, and greatly rewarded, such as have opposed themselves to their ungodly commandments and blind rage.

questions.question: How does Bradley Pierce frame the choice between obeying the Supreme Court and obeying God?
questions.answer: In his Dobbs amicus brief, Pierce argues that demanding unconditional submission turns the Court into a tyrant, and granting it makes the Court an idol, framing nullification as both a constitutional and a first-commandment issue.
questions.quote: If the Court demands unconditional submission, it makes itself a tyrant. If we grant it unconditional submission, we make it an idol.

questions.question: What does the author cite as evidence that even abortion supporters concede Roe v. Wade is constitutionally indefensible?
questions.answer: He quotes Stanford Law's John Hart Ely calling Roe not constitutional law, and Harry Blackmun's clerk Edward Lazarus admitting in the Washington Post that most liberal jurisprudes would, under truth serum, call Roe basically indefensible.
questions.quote: as a matter of constitutional interpretation, even most liberal jurisprudes — if you administer truth serum — will tell you [*Roe*] is basically indefensible.

questions.question: What historical precedent does the article offer for state-level nullification of unjust federal law?
questions.answer: Silberman points to the slavery abolitionists, including the Wisconsin Supreme Court voiding the Fugitive Slave Act, Massachusetts' 1855 Personal Liberty Act criminalizing its enforcement, and similar acts in Vermont and Michigan, plus New York jury nullification.
questions.quote: Massachusetts’ Personal Liberty Act of 1855 made enforcing the Fugitive Slave Act a crime punishable by up to five years in prison, mandated impeachment for any state official who aided slave-catchers, and disbarred any attorney assisting in the return of slaves to their “owners.”

questions.question: How does the article connect 'just following orders' to today's pro-life officials?
questions.answer: Silberman invokes the Nuremberg Defense used by Adolf Eichmann and other Nazi war criminals and says it is no more legitimate for pro-life governors, legislators, and judges who continue to allow abortion under the cover of higher court rulings.
questions.quote: The Nuremberg Defense is no more legitimate for the pro-life Governors, State legislators, and State judges sanctioning the mass murder of preborn children today than it was for those on trial in Nuremberg.

questions.question: Which specific Republican officials does the author name as needing to be politically ended?
questions.answer: He names Arkansas Governor Asa Hutchinson for vetoing the Arkansas Sovereignty Act and Oklahoma Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat for opposing the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act since 2019, calling for the end of their influence in the GOP.
questions.quote: Governor Asa Hutchinson’s veto of the [Arkansas Sovereignty Act](https://www.arkleg.state.ar.us/Bills/Detail?id=SB298&ddBienniumSession=2021/2021R) must be the end of his career as an influential voice in the Republican party.

questions.question: What does the author ultimately say the question of nullification reveals about the people who refuse it?
questions.answer: He frames the issue as one of character rather than mere politics, citing Pierce's rhetorical question about Americans unwilling to say no to evil, and answering it bluntly: it makes us evil.
questions.quote: If Americans are unwilling to say no to evil, what does that make us?

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Foundational explainer of the nullification doctrine that this article applies as a Republican litmus test.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: Expands on the historical case against the myth that nullifiers were villains, central to Silberman's third argument.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: Background on Greg Treat, one of the two officials Silberman calls to be politically ended.

related.title: Contrary to Treat's Media Tour, SB13 is Not a Secession Bill
related.reason: Continues the argument against Treat's specific objections to nullification-style abolition legislation.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Examines another pro-life official's denial of nullification, the same posture Silberman critiques here.

related.title: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
related.reason: Reports on the Bradley Pierce Dobbs amicus brief that Silberman quotes throughout this piece.

related.title: 4 Abolitionist Rapid Reactions to the (Wrongly Decided) Dobbs Decision
related.reason: Follows up on the Dobbs case Silberman previews, showing how the call for nullification persisted post-decision.
